questions.description: "The authority to bind a company can arise through a director having ostensible authority, and third parties seeking to…
questions.text: "The authority to bind a company can arise through a director having ostensible authority, and third parties seeking to enforce contracts against companies entered into by directors may also rely upon the Rule in Turquand's Case and section 40 of the Companies Act 2014." You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: "The key principle in Irish company law is that a company is a legal person, separate and distinct from its members and…
questions.text: "The key principle in Irish company law is that a company is a legal person, separate and distinct from its members and directors." You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Paul is a director of Car4All Limited (the "Company") which operates a car… Fiduciary, Company.
questions.text: Paul is a director of Car4All Limited (the "Company") which operates a car sales business in South County Dublin. Paul is not a shareholder in the Company which is owned by a family consortium headed-up by Simon, although Paul has worked in the Company for 10 years. He worked first as a car salesman before being promoted on a number of occasions until finally being appointed a director of the Company with responsibility for sales. Simon, the majority shareholder and Managing Director of the Company, approaches you for advice. It has come to his knowledge that Paul has been "double-jobbing" for the last two years selling second-hand cars through a website fronted by his wife, Jackie. Simon heard from a friend that Paul has been buying cars that were traded in to the Company at a discounted price and selling them on for a significant profit on his website. He also used the Company's workshop to service them before re-sale, again availing of discounts on the time and materials charged. You are required to advise Simon on the law relating to directors' common law and equitable fiduciary duties now contained in section 233 of the Companies Act 2014 and on the Company's remedies against Paul and Jackie. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law and apply these to the facts provided above.

questions.description: William's World of Colours Limited (the "Company") is a company engaged in… Company, Director.
questions.text: William's World of Colours Limited (the "Company") is a company engaged in the business of manufacturing and distributing paint to the Irish construction industry. Fort the past number of years, business has been booming. Two of the Company's customers have had lucrative contracts for the painting of new housing estates. The Company has three directors -- William, Peggy and Eugene -- and is a single-member private limited company, all shares being owned by William. William is the son of the Company's founder and is the managing director. Peggy, his daughter, is the Sales Director and Eugene is her husband. Eugene is a non-executive director who works as a local self-employed solicitor. His involvement with the Company is very limited and he never attends meetings of the directors. In recent times the Company's fortunes have faltered. Both of its major customers have gone into liquidation, owing the Company several hundred thousand euro. Both customers were always slow payers, but the Company received nothing from them for months although it continued to supply them with paint. William realised things were bad when the Company's bank manager telephoned him to say that the Company had exceeded its overdraft. William persuaded the bank to extend the overdraft to pay wages and give him time to collect some outstanding debts. The Company chugged along for another month but then William realised that the Company was insolvent and without any hope of an immediate improvement. At that point William placed the Company into creditors' voluntary liquidation. The Company's liquidator discovered that the Company's record keeping was sporadic, and that management accounts had not been prepared with the frequency which would have alerted the Company's directors to the fact that its two main customers had not been discharging what was owed by them for some time. You are required to advise on the likelihood that each of the three directors will be made the subject of a restriction order pursuant to Section 819 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statue law.

questions.description: "Although section 212 of the Companies Act 2014 provides a powerful remedy for oppressed shareholders, the exceptions…
questions.text: "Although section 212 of the Companies Act 2014 provides a powerful remedy for oppressed shareholders, the exceptions to the Rule in Foss v Harbottle and the derivative action continue to be important mechanisms for holding errant directors to account." You are required to discuss this statement. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Gordon is the liquidator of Fabulous Foods Limited (the "Company") which… Company, Director.
questions.text: Gordon is the liquidator of Fabulous Foods Limited (the "Company") which has been placed into official liquidation following the bringing of a petition by the revenue commissioners grounded on the Company's inability to pay its debts. Gordon seeks your advice on the validity of the following transactions: Eighteen months before the presentation of the petition, the Company had created a floating charge in favour of its sole member and managing director, John, to secure loan of €50,000 made by John to the Company. There is evidence that the loan made by John was advanced in cash the day after the floating charge was created. Two cheques were drawn and dated one month before the petition was presented to the High Court to have the Company would up. The first cheque for €15,000 was payable to John and represented the repayment of part of the loan made 18 months earlier. This cheque was cashed the same day it was drawn. The second cheque for €30,000 was payable to a creditor of the company for goods purchased by the Company, and it was cashed the day after the presentation was presented. You are required to advise Gordon on the transactions described in (a) and (b) and in your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "The most significant changes introduced by Part 7 of the Companies Act 2014 to the registration of charges by…
questions.text: "The most significant changes introduced by Part 7 of the Companies Act 2014 to the registration of charges by companies is not the new "two-stage procedure" but is, rather, the changes of the types of security that are now required to be registered." You are required to discuss this statement. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "In addition to consolidating the law, the Companies Act 2014 has introduced significant reforms to Irish Company law."…
questions.text: "In addition to consolidating the law, the Companies Act 2014 has introduced significant reforms to Irish Company law." You are required to identify and discuss the implications of what you view to be the five main reforms introduced by the Companies Act 2014. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law in support of your views and you must not include a change that you have already addressed in another answer.

questions.description: Compare and contrast the key features of, and differences between,…
questions.text: Compare and contrast the key features of, and differences between, companies limited by shares to which Parts 2 to 14 apply (i.e. LTDs) and designated activity companies (i.e. DACs). In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Corporate Systems Ltd (the "Company") is a company limited by shares to… Consideration, Company.
questions.text: Corporate Systems Ltd (the "Company") is a company limited by shares to which Parts 2 to 14 of the Companies Act 2014 (the "Act") applies. You are approached for advice by Richard who recently purchased the Company's issued share capital from Jason and Janet for €3m; Jason transferred to Richard 100 "A" shares held by him, and Janet transferred to Richard 100 "B" shares held by her. After acquiring the Company's shares, Richard discovered that Jason, who had also been the sole director of the Company, had caused the Company to do the following things the week before the sale of the shares took place: 1. An interim dividend in the amount of €200,000 was declared and paid on the "B" shares held by Janet, notwithstanding that the Company had no distributable reserves as required by section 1 17(1) of the Act; 2. A site owned by the Company with an open market value of €200,000 was transferred to Jason for the consideration of €1 ,000. Richard seeks your advice on the foregoing matters, and asks whether the Company can set them aside and the legal basis upon which he could cause the Company to go about setting them aside. You are required to advise Richard and in your answer you must set out all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "The introduction of the Summary Approval Procedure contained in Chapter 7 of Part 4 of the Companies Act 2014 has…
questions.text: "The introduction of the Summary Approval Procedure contained in Chapter 7 of Part 4 of the Companies Act 2014 has resulted in a unified approach to permitting companies to engage in activities that would otherwise be prohibited provided that certain safeguards are met. The key safeguards are that the members of the company must resolve in favour of the company engaging in the activity and that the directors must make a declaration of solvency." You are required to discuss this statement, having particular regard to the rationale for the two safeguards referred to in the statement. In your answer you must cite ail relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "Section 602 of the Companies Act 2014 is a statutory example of the pari passu principle which requires all creditors…
questions.text: "Section 602 of the Companies Act 2014 is a statutory example of the pari passu principle which requires all creditors of an insolvent company to be placed on an equal footing and not "leap frog" the statutory queue ordained by the Companies Acts". You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Richard and Frank are equal shareholders and directors in RF Events… Mortgage, Company.
questions.text: Richard and Frank are equal shareholders and directors in RF Events Limited (the "Company") which has been in the business of organising corporate events since 2005. Business was very strong for the last number of years and while the Company has a core staff of 12 employees, at times it employed up to 30 people. In the last year business has never been worse. Clients are either not hosting events or are cancelling events already planned. Richard and Frank have cut staff salaries by 20% and have stopped taking a salary for themselves. Even so, the Company is struggling to keep afloat and is behind on both the rent for its premises and its bank loan of some €2,500,000. The bank loan is secured by a mortgage debenture incorporating a floating charge and a fixed charge on the Company's premises, which is now worth only €1, 750,000. This month it is unlikely to be able to pay to the Revenue Commissioners the full amount of VAT it collected. Richard and Frank believe that if the Company can only keep afloat for the next 6 months, it stands a good chance of surviving as they believe the worst of the recession is over. Frank has approached an old business colleague, now retired, and asked him to become an investor in the company, offering him 25% of the Company (12 1h% each from him and Richard). Matters came to a head yesterday when the Company's bank appointed a receiver on account of the Company's bank loan being in arrears. Richard and Frank approach you for advice on: (a) the steps involved in bringing a petition to have an examiner appointed to the Company under Part 10 of the Companies Act 2014; (b) the likelihood of success of a petition to have an examiner appointed to the (c) the consequence for the Company and its creditors of an examiner being appointed. You are required to advise Richard and Frank in relation to the matters set out at (a), (b) and (c) which should be clearly distinguished in your answer. You are required to refer to all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Delia's Dahlias Limited (the "Company") has operated a successful florist… Company, Windingup.
questions.text: Delia's Dahlias Limited (the "Company") has operated a successful florist shop in Limerick city for the last thirty years. The Company was founded by Delia but last year Delia retired from the business and transferred her shares in the Company to her daughters, Daphne and Doris, who now hold the shares in the Company 50:50 each. It was as if Delia was a buffer between Daphne and Doris, for although the shop was often tense as the two sisters asserted their own ideas as to the future of the shop, relations proceeded on a reasonably even keel. While Daphne has been happy to develop the business as essentially a retail operation attracting passing customers, building regulars and internet customers, Doris has for some time been keen on developing a wholesale business to supply hotels for weddings and undertakers for funerals. She wants ultimately to sell the shop and cash in on the property boom. When Delia retired the sisters' relationship deteriorated rapidly and led ultimately to a screaming match in front of a shop full of customers; Daphne had sold the last orchid to a customer which Doris had set aside for a wedding order placed by a hotel with which she was trying to develop a relationship. Since then Daphne has not passed on messages to Doris from hotels and has been hanging up the telephone on undertakers, whom she believes too creepy to converse with. For months, the sisters have not been on speaking terms, and last week Doris sent Daphne a solicitor's letter wherein Doris claimed oppression by Daphne. The letter threatens the institution of legal proceedings for oppression and the winding up of the Company unless Daphne agrees to sell her 50% interest in the Company's share capital for a price to be determined as its market value by the Company's auditors. You are required to advise Daphne as to the law applicable to oppression under section 212 of the Companies Act 2014 and winding-up on just and equitable ground under section 569(1)(e) of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Big Bank plc (the "Bank") made a loan of €5m to Mighty Motors Limited (the… Company.
questions.text: Big Bank plc (the "Bank") made a loan of €5m to Mighty Motors Limited (the "Company") on the security of a fixed charge over its car showroom and garage in Tallaght County Dublin and a floating charge over its undertaking and assets. The security document provides that upon the passing of a resolution or presentation of a petition to wind up the company, the floating charge will crystallise. After the Bank failed to make any payments to the Revenue Commissioners for three months, the Revenue Commissioners presented a petition to have the Company wound up. You have been approached by the liquidator who tells you that the Bank is claiming to have a fixed charge over 20 high value second-hand cars which were on the Company's premises, on the grounds that the floating charge crystallised when the Revenue Commissioners presented the petition to have the Company wound up. The Revenue Commissioners are however claiming that they are entitled to the cars on the grounds that they have priority over floating charges. You are required to advise the liquidator on the foregoing matter. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Pat is a plasterer from Palmerstown.
questions.text: Pat is a plasterer from Palmerstown. He operates as a sole trader and employs two staff. They work with Pat plastering new build houses and extensions. Recently, Peter, one of Pat's employees, narrowly avoided serious injury when the stilts he was wearing snagged on a hole in the floor. Only for Pat being in close proximity and able to catch him, the employee could have been seriously injured as he would have fallen into an open basement on the property and plunged four metres to the ground. This near miss has caused Pat to consider whether being a sole trader is such a good idea. Additionally, Paul, Pat's other employee, has been pressing Pat to give him an interest in the business. Pat approaches you for advice, seeking information on (a) the advantages and disadvantages of forming a company and (b) the consequences of incorporation for Pat's business. You are required to answer both (a) and (b) and in your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.
